text: with a history of ITP, who was diagnosed almost a year prior to this operation. He underwent many conservative medical therapy with no success. Therefore, he had an indication to have robotic-assisted espermectomy.

The patient is positioned in the right lateral position. The surgeon uses robotic arms and an assist pro-car to lift the spleen, retracting the hilum. The peritoneal attachments are carefully dissected layer by layer to expose the splenic artery, while cautiously avoiding the tip of the pancreas located nearby.

The surgeon uses fluorescent technology to visualize the splenic vessels, allowing for precise dissection and isolation of these vessels. The vessels are clearly identified and safely dissected around.
The surgeon clips each vessel individually and then cuts them, ensuring precise isolation and division.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to quickly and bloodlessly divide the gastroesophageal ligaments containing the short gastric vessels, while protecting the stomach and lateral peritoneal attachments of the spleen, including the colon and ligaments.

The surgeon irrigates the splenic bed to confirm hemostasis after the spleen is completely resected. A T-cell bio-glue and hemostatic agent is applied to enhance hemostasis. The resected spleen is then placed into a large retrieval bag.
